displayTitle: When the Camera Hits the Road
kicker: RAD SAFETY // SHIPPING SOURCES · TYPE B · PLACARDS

beats.narration: The moment a loaded camera leaves the site, you stop being just a radiographer and become a hazmat shipper, answerable to two rulebooks at once.

payload.title: WHEN THE CAMERA HITS THE ROAD
payload.sub: Two rulebooks. One survey. Four placards.

beats.narration: Two agencies split the work. NRC certifies the package; DOT governs the move. A working iridium source exceeds A-one, so the camera ships Type B.
Survey the locked package first. The highest one-meter reading, rounded up to the next tenth, becomes the transport index. The surface reading and TI pick the label.

payload.heading: Survey first, the label writes itself
payload.note: 1 m READING ↑ NEXT TENTH = TI · SURFACE + TI PICK THE LABEL

beats.narration: A Yellow-Three package puts radioactive placards on all four sides. The papers carry the UN number, nuclide, activity, and a twenty-four hour number, riding within reach.

chips.s: Placards on each side and end
On the seat when you step out

beats.narration: Block it, brace it, keep it under constant surveillance, and deliver it by procedure. The survey decides everything else.

payload.sub: Locked, plugged, surveyed — block, brace, and never leave it unwatched.
